However, the convergence property of Algorithm 3.1 is not known when there are infinitely many KKT points. In Example 5.2(ii), there are infinitely many KKT points that are not NEs, but Algorithm 3.1 is still able to get an NE in a few loops. If there are infinitely many KKT points that are not NEs, does Algorithm 3.1 still converge to find an NE? This question is mostly open to the authors.
title: Prove convergence of iterative KKT-point exclusion for nonconvex polynomial Nash equilibria
background_latex:
Consider a Nash equilibrium problem with \(N\) players. Player \(i\) chooses a strategy vector \(x_i\in\mathbb{R}^{n_i}\) and faces an optimization problem parameterized by the other players' strategies \(x_{-i}\):
\[
\min_{x_i\in\mathbb{R}^{n_i}} f_i(x_i,x_{-i}) \quad\text{s.t.}\quad g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\ (j\in E_i),\quad g_{i,j}(x_i)\ge 0\ (j\in I_i),
\]
where \(f_i\) is a polynomial in \(x=(x_1,\dots,x_N)\) and each constraint polynomial \(g_{i,j}\) depends only on \(x_i\). The feasible set of player \(i\) is
\[
X_i := \{x_i\in\mathbb{R}^{n_i}: g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\ (j\in E_i),\ g_{i,j}(x_i)\ge 0\ (j\in I_i)\},
\]
and the joint feasible set is \(X:=X_1\times\cdots\times X_N\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\), \(n:=\sum_i n_i\).

A (pure-strategy) Nash equilibrium is a point \(x^*\in X\) such that for every \(i\), \(x_i^*\) is a global minimizer of player \(i\)'s problem at \(x_{-i}^*\).

Assume each constraint tuple \(g_i:=(g_{i,1},\dots,g_{i,m_i})\) is \"nonsingular\" in the sense that a certain associated polynomial matrix has full column rank over \(\mathbb{C}^{n_i}\); under this assumption, the Lagrange multipliers for the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions can be expressed as polynomial functions \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\). Then every Nash equilibrium satisfies a polynomial system encoding the KKT equalities together with complementarity and sign conditions:
1) stationarity \(\nabla_{x_i} f_i(x) - \sum_{j=1}^{m_i} \lambda_{i,j}(x)\nabla_{x_i} g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\),
2) primal feasibility \(g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\) for \(j\in E_i\), \(g_{i,j}(x_i)\ge 0\) for \(j\in I_i\),
3) complementarity \(\lambda_{i,j}(x) g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\) for \(j\in I_i\),
4) dual feasibility \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\ge 0\) for \(j\in I_i\).

The paper proposes an iterative algorithm (Algorithm 3.1) that repeatedly solves a polynomial optimization problem over the KKT-feasible set (using a generic strictly convex quadratic objective \([x]_1^\top\Theta [x]_1\) to pick a unique candidate) and then checks whether the candidate is a Nash equilibrium by solving each player's best-response problem. If the candidate \(u\) is not an equilibrium, the algorithm adds finitely many additional polynomial inequalities of the form
\[
f_i(v,x_{-i})-f_i(x_i,x_{-i})\ge 0
\]
for selected best-response points \(v\) (collected in sets \(K_i\)) to exclude the found non-equilibrium KKT point(s) and repeats.
problem_definition_latex:
Assume the nonsingularity condition holds so that polynomial Lagrange multiplier expressions \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\) exist, and suppose a Nash equilibrium exists.

Analyze Algorithm 3.1 described above in the case where there are infinitely many KKT points that are not Nash equilibria. Determine whether, under such circumstances, the iterative procedure of repeatedly:
1) solving the current polynomial optimization problem over the KKT-feasible set augmented with the accumulated inequalities indexed by \(K_i\),
2) checking the resulting optimizer \(u\) for the Nash equilibrium property via each player's global best-response computation,
3) enlarging \(K_i\) by adding some best-response optimizer(s) when \(u\) fails the Nash equilibrium check,
necessarily converges (in finitely or infinitely many iterations) to a Nash equilibrium.

Equivalently: give conditions under which Algorithm 3.1 is guaranteed to find a Nash equilibrium (or certify nonexistence) even when the set of non-equilibrium KKT points is infinite, or provide an explicit example showing failure of convergence.

2) checking the resulting optimizer \(u\) for the Nash equilibrium property via each player's global best-response computation,

location_in_paper: Section 6 (Conclusions and Discussions), page 26 (arXiv v2 pdf pagination), near the end of the section beginning 'There is much interesting future work to do.'
open_problem_quote: However, the convergence property of Algorithm 3.1 is not known when there are infinitely many KKT points. In Example 5.2(ii), there are infinitely many KKT points that are not NEs, but Algorithm 3.1 is still able to get an NE in a few loops. If there are infinitely many KKT points that are not NEs, does Algorithm 3.1 still converge to find an NE? This question is mostly open to the authors.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can algorithmically generate small polynomial games with low degrees and dimensions and test Algorithm 3.1 computationally for cycling or divergence, but the search space is large and the outcome depends on implementation choices (e.g., which best-response optimizers are added to the sets \(K_i\)). Still, convergence or failure can often be checked empirically on finite-dimensional instances.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate counterexamples (families of polynomial games) and algorithm variants (rules for selecting points added to \(K_i\), choice of \(\Theta\), etc.) can be encoded and scored by running the resulting procedure and measuring progress toward an equilibrium or detecting nontermination. The main limitation is computational cost of repeated Moment-SOS solves and the difficulty of designing a fitness signal that correlates with provable nonconvergence rather than just slow convergence on sampled instances.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In the NEPP framework of Nie–Tang, nonsingularity of each player’s constraint tuple yields polynomial Lagrange-multiplier expressions \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\), so every Nash equilibrium lies in the semialgebraic KKT-feasible set \(G\) of (3.7). Algorithm 3.1 then runs a select–verify–cut loop: select a unique KKT point by minimizing a generic strictly convex quadratic \([x]_1^\top\Theta[x]_1\) over the current relaxation (3.10), verify equilibrium by solving each best-response global problem (3.8), and cut off the current non-equilibrium KKT point by adding inequalities \(f_i(v,x_{-i})-f_i(x_i,x_{-i})\ge 0\) for best-response witnesses \(v\in K_i\). The original paper proves finite termination when \(|G\setminus G^*|<\infty\) (Theorem 3.2) and shows that this finiteness holds generically because the complex KKT system is zero-dimensional (Theorem A.1), but explicitly leaves open the nongeneric regime where \(G\setminus G^*\) is infinite (Section 6).

Forward-citation work provides partial progress by establishing asymptotic convergence of analogous exclusion loops under additional regularity assumptions. For rational generalized Nash equilibrium problems, the rational analogue proves that if spurious KKT points are infinite, then any accumulation point of the selected iterates is a (generalized) Nash equilibrium provided certain strict-feasibility and continuity hypotheses hold (notably continuity of each player’s value function and uniform continuity of the feasible-extension maps). For polynomial variational inequalities, an essentially identical KKT-exclusion scheme yields that, in the infinite-spurious-KKT regime, any accumulation point is a true VI solution under continuity of an appropriate gap function at the limit and boundedness of selected auxiliary minimizers. These results suggest that unconditional convergence of Algorithm 3.1 is unlikely without extra assumptions; rather, one should expect convergence of accumulation points under continuity/compactness-type conditions ensuring that the separating inequalities do not “chase” a positive-dimensional set of spurious KKT points without approaching \(G^*\).

Other related papers mainly reinforce the generic-finiteness escape hatch or provide alternative computational routes. Algebraic-degree results for (generalized) Nash problems quantify generic finiteness of complex Fritz–John/KKT points, while polyhedral homotopy methods exploit this to enumerate all KKT tuples in the zero-dimensional (generic) case and then verify equilibria. SOS-based select–verify–cut methods in structured polynomial game classes (e.g., imperfect-recall games) prove asymptotic exactness of the overall loop, again pointing toward asymptotic (rather than finite) guarantees when the KKT set is not finite. Overall, the specific open question for nonconvex polynomial NEPPs with infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points remains open: existing progress indicates that convergence can be proved for accumulation points under additional continuity/feasibility assumptions, but no general theorem (or definitive counterexample) is currently established for Algorithm 3.1 in full generality.

sources.title: Rational generalized Nash equilibrium problems
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proposes Algorithm 3.3 (a rational-function analogue of the KKT-point exclusion scheme) and proves: - Finite termination if the set of non-equilibrium KKT points is finite (Theorem 3.6). - If non-equilibrium KKT points are infinite, any accumulation point of the iterates is a GNE provided (i) strict-inequality constraints stay strictly feasible at the limit, (ii) each player’s value function \(\nu_i(x_{-i})=\inf_{x_i\in X_i(x_{-i})} f_i(x_i,x_{-i})\) is continuous at the limit, and (iii) the sequence of feasible extensions \(p_i^{(k)}\) is uniformly continuous at the limit (Theorem 3.7).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for convergence guarantees (or counterexamples) for the iterative KKT-point exclusion Algorithm 3.1 for nonconvex polynomial NEPs when \(K\setminus S\) is infinite. This citing paper directly addresses the analogous difficulty (in a more general rGNEP setting) and gives an asymptotic convergence theorem (Theorem 3.7) precisely for the case \(|K\setminus S|=\infty\), under additional regularity assumptions (continuity of value functions and uniform continuity of the exclusion maps/feasible extensions). It also provides examples where \(K\setminus S\) is infinite yet the algorithm terminates finitely (Example 6.1(i),(ii)), showing that infinitude alone does not preclude convergence. However, it does not prove unconditional convergence for arbitrary nonconvex polynomial NEPs with infinite non-equilibrium KKT points; the result depends on assumptions (notably existence of feasible extensions and continuity conditions) and is formulated for generalized Nash problems with coupling constraints and rational LMEs.

sources.title: Solving polynomial variational inequality problems via Lagrange multiplier expressions and Moment-SOS relaxations
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proposes Algorithm 3.2 for polynomial variational inequalities VI\((X,F)\) with polynomial feasible set \(X=\{g_i\ge0, g_i=0\}\) under a nonsingularity assumption ensuring polynomial Lagrange multiplier expressions \(\lambda(x)\). Let \(K\) be the semialgebraic set of KKT points expressed purely in \(x\) via LMEs (system (2.8)), and let \(s:=|K\setminus \mathrm{SOL}(X,F)|\). Theorem 3.4 proves: (i) if \(\mathrm{SOL}(X,F)=\emptyset\) and \(s<\infty\), Algorithm 3.2 detects nonexistence in at most \(s+1\) loops; (ii) if \(\mathrm{SOL}(X,F)\neq\emptyset\) and \(s<\infty\), it finds a solution in at most \(s+1\) loops; (iii) if \(s=\infty\) and the algorithm does not finitely terminate, then any accumulation point \(u^*\) of iterates \(u^{(k)}\) is a solution provided the gap function \(\nu(u)=\inf_{y\in X}(y-u)^T F(u)\) is continuous at \(u^*\) and \(\limsup_{k\to\infty}T^{(k)}\) (minimizers of the gap subproblem) is bounded. Additionally, Theorem 3.5 shows that for generic polynomial data \(F,g\), the KKT set \(K\) is finite, implying finite termination generically.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for convergence guarantees of an iterative KKT-point exclusion scheme (Algorithm 3.1 in the Nash equilibrium paper) in the difficult case where there are infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points. This citing paper studies an essentially identical exclusion scheme for nonconvex polynomial VIPs: it repeatedly optimizes a generic strictly convex quadratic over the KKT-feasible set \(K\), checks true VI feasibility via a global gap minimization, and adds separating inequalities \((v-x)^T F(x)\ge0\) to exclude the current non-solution KKT point. Theorem 3.4(iii) directly addresses the infinite-KKT-points regime: it does not prove finite termination, but gives asymptotic convergence of accumulation points to true solutions under extra assumptions (gap-function continuity at the limit point and boundedness of selected minimizers). It also proves finite termination when the number of spurious KKT points is finite, and shows this finiteness holds generically. While the paper is for VIPs rather than Nash equilibria, polynomial Nash equilibria can be formulated as polynomial VIPs (e.g., via Nikaido–Isoda/VI formulations under suitable conditions), so the results provide partial progress and a template of conditions for convergence in the infinite-spurious-KKT setting, but do not fully resolve the Nash-equilibrium-specific open problem in full generality.

sources.title: Solving Imperfect-Recall Games via Sum-of-Squares Optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proposes SOS-based algorithms (following Nie & Tang, 2024) for behavioral Nash equilibria in imperfect-recall extensive-form games by reducing to polynomial games over products of simplices and applying a Select–Verify–Cut (SVC) loop over the joint KKT system. Main guarantee (Theorem 5.1): (i) SVC is asymptotically exact—if a behavioral NE exists, then as the Moment/SOS relaxation order and number of SVC iterations grow, the method returns an NE; otherwise it certifies nonexistence. (ii) Under generic utility coefficients, each SOS subproblem has finite convergence and the SVC loop terminates in finitely many iterations. (iii) In non-absentminded games, a single vertex-restricted selector program suffices; its SOS hierarchy is asymptotically exact and generically finitely convergent.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for convergence of an iterative KKT-point exclusion algorithm (Algorithm 3.1 in Nie–Tang’s polynomial Nash equilibrium paper) in the difficult case where there are infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points. This citing paper studies essentially the same SVC/select-verify-cut paradigm specialized to imperfect-recall extensive-form games (a subclass of polynomial games over products of simplices) and proves asymptotic convergence without requiring finiteness of the non-NE KKT set (Theorem 5.1(i)). It also proves finite termination under a genericity assumption implying the joint KKT system has finitely many solutions (Theorem 5.1(ii)), which excludes the ‘infinitely many KKT points’ regime. Thus it gives partial progress: it provides conditions (generic utilities; or structural NAM case with vertex restrictions) guaranteeing finite termination, and it asserts asymptotic exactness more generally, but it does not resolve the open question of whether the original Algorithm 3.1 necessarily converges when the set of non-equilibrium KKT points is infinite, nor does it provide a counterexample of failure in that regime.

sources.title: Algebraic degrees of generalized Nash equilibrium problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Under genericity assumptions on the defining polynomials of a (generalized) Nash equilibrium problem, the paper proves the Fritz–John system has finitely many complex solutions and, generically, every Fritz–John point is a KKT point (Theorem 3.1). It further gives explicit algebraic-degree formulae counting these complex Fritz–John/KKT points for given active sets and summed over all active sets (Theorems 5.1–5.3), and upper bounds for nongeneric cases (Theorem 5.4).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns convergence/termination of an iterative algorithm that excludes non-equilibrium KKT points when there may be infinitely many such KKT points. This paper does not analyze Algorithm 3.1 or exclusion-based convergence. However, it establishes that for generic polynomial data the set of complex Fritz–John points (hence KKT points) is finite. In such generic regimes, the problematic scenario of infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points cannot occur, and one can bound the number of candidate KKT points that any exclusion-based enumeration method would ever need to consider. For nongeneric data, the paper provides only degree upper bounds when the Fritz–John set is finite, but does not give conditions ensuring finiteness of real KKT points or guarantee that iterative exclusion will reach a Nash equilibrium when infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points exist.

sources.title: On the polyhedral homotopy method for solving generalized Nash equilibrium problems of polynomials
sources.theorem_or_result: Under genericity assumptions (dense generic polynomial data), the complex KKT system for a polynomial GNEP is Bernstein generic, so its mixed volume equals its algebraic degree; consequently polyhedral homotopy continuation can compute all complex KKT tuples, and Algorithm 3.1 then returns all generalized Nash equilibria (or certifies none exist) provided all complex KKT tuples are found (e.g., when the number found equals the mixed volume).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for convergence guarantees of an iterative KKT-point exclusion scheme (Algorithm 3.1 in Nie–Tang’s NEP paper) in the difficult regime where there are infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points. This citing paper proposes a different framework: compute (generically finitely many) complex KKT tuples via polyhedral homotopy, then filter true equilibria by solving best-response polynomial optimizations via Moment-SOS. Their correctness/termination relies on finiteness of the KKT solution set (zero-dimensional KKT system) under genericity; they explicitly note that if there are infinitely many complex KKT points, their method ‘may still find one or more GNEs’ but they do not prove completeness or convergence in that case. Thus it does not resolve the open question about guaranteed convergence/exclusion when the non-equilibrium KKT set is infinite; rather, it provides tools and generic conditions that avoid the infinite-KKT regime.

sources.title: Convex generalized Nash equilibrium problems and polynomial optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: For convex generalized Nash equilibrium problems with polynomial data (GNEPPs), the paper gives a polynomial-optimization reformulation of the GNE conditions using rational or parametric expressions for KKT multipliers, and proves convergence/correctness of a Moment-SOS based algorithm: under standard constraint qualifications ensuring every GNE is a KKT point and under positivity of the multiplier-expression denominators (or using parametric expressions), solving the resulting polynomial optimization (via the Moment-SOS hierarchy) returns a generalized Nash equilibrium if one exists, or certifies nonexistence when the KKT-feasible set is empty (Theorems 5.2, 5.4, 5.6).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns a nonconvex polynomial NEP algorithm (Algorithm 3.1 in Nie–Tang’s NEP paper) that iteratively excludes non-equilibrium KKT points by adding best-response inequalities, and asks for convergence guarantees or counterexamples when there are infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points. The citing paper does not analyze that nonconvex exclusion scheme. Instead it treats convex polynomial GNEPs, where KKT points are already equilibria, so the ‘infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points’ pathology cannot occur in the same way. It does, however, develop closely related machinery (rational/parametric multiplier expressions; Moment-SOS solution of KKT polynomial systems; uniqueness via generic quadratic objective) and explicitly states that extending the exclusion idea to nonconvex GNEPs/NEPs and detecting nonexistence when the KKT system is feasible are open (Section 7). Thus it provides tools and partial conceptual guidance but not a convergence proof for the nonconvex iterative KKT-point exclusion algorithm.

sources.title: Nash Equilibrium Problems of Polynomials
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proposes Algorithm 3.1 for computing Nash equilibria of polynomial Nash equilibrium problems (NEPPs) via Moment-SOS relaxations and iterative exclusion of non-equilibrium KKT points, and proves finite termination when the set of non-equilibrium KKT points is finite: if \(G\) is the KKT-feasible set of (3.7) and \(G^*\) the set of Nash equilibria, then if \(|G\setminus G^*|<\infty\), Algorithm 3.1 terminates in at most \(|G\setminus G^*|\) loops (Theorem 3.2). Under genericity assumptions on the polynomial data, the KKT system has finitely many complex solutions (Theorem A.1), hence Algorithm 3.1 terminates finitely and either finds an NE or detects nonexistence (Theorem 3.3). The paper explicitly states that convergence of Algorithm 3.1 is not understood when there are infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points (Section 6).
sources.connection: This citing paper is the source of the open problem: it proves convergence/finite termination only under finiteness of \(G\setminus G^*\) (or genericity implying finitely many KKT points), and it explicitly leaves open the case where there are infinitely many KKT points that are not Nash equilibria. It provides an example (Example 5.2(ii)) where infinitely many non-NE KKT points exist yet Algorithm 3.1 still finds an NE quickly, but it does not give general conditions guaranteeing convergence nor a counterexample showing failure. Thus it restates and motivates exactly the open question posed.
